values.QID20_TEXT: Union employees' lack of collaboration with MPPs and their efforts to work as little as possible (as a whole - there are few exceptions); Unprofessionalism and lack of leadership, collaboration, and support at the college (COTA) level; lack of leadership from the university to support COTA and model support for development on the academic side of the house.
values.QID21_TEXT: Stop being beholden to the unions. They are demoralizing to productive employees. Also, foster a culture of philanthropy throughout all divisions; this will be the only chance to increase fundraising ultimately.

values.QID20_TEXT: Not having more flexibility with workload.
values.QID21_TEXT: The ability to have a voice in general.  Being at the support level, things fall to us to take care of.  We could use more communication regarding what it available.  We usually find out from other Departments what they are doing.
values.QID29_TEXT: Retiring from this position as I have had  two jobs since being here at CSULB.  About 5 years ago my other job changed with much more responsibility, due to someone else relinquishing their duties there.  This other position at a medical office position is at night and weekends.  I never have had time for regular relaxation like most of society, especially since that breakdown at the other job.  I am retiring from this position now since I am of retirement age and feel it is a good time for me.  I will continue to work at the medical office until finally completely retiring which could be 3-5 years.  It is dependent on the doctor and the business there when he closes the office, to be determined.  One job at this point in my life is better for my quality of life.

values.QID19_TEXT: the mission of helping the students

values.QID29_TEXT: Pay

values.QID19_TEXT: The people, the staff I worked with were wonderful. Caring and compassionate people who cared about the students and their jobs.
values.QID20_TEXT: The administration and leadership tell staff that they care about employees but when it comes to making decisions and that can positively affect our work environment and make retention better they ignore the feedback and requests. The workload is atrocious. Staff are doing the work of multiple people because administrators do not want to hire more people. Sadly this workload does not come with flexibility or a better salary. Implementation of the remote policy has been inequitable as well. I am leaving because of lack of flexibility, low pay, and unrealistic workloads.
values.QID21_TEXT: Provide more flexibility to staff, especially during slower times at the University like summer. Embrace the remote policy, currently the University and Colleges are grudgingly going with the policy but making it as difficult as possible to implement. Also, hire more people in each department, you can talk about mental health all you want but when it comes down to it I the amount of work that staff are given is not reasonable, especially for the sub-par wages we receive. It's very shameful that the University and CSU as a whole takes advantage of theitlr employees.
values.QID29_TEXT: Workload, flexibility, and pay. I am returning to the UC system for more pay, more flexibility, and a managable workload.

values.QID19_TEXT: The community of workers made me always feel welcome
values.QID20_TEXT: N/A
values.QID21_TEXT: N/A
values.QID29_TEXT: Starting school up so against University policy

values.QID19_TEXT: Staff. There are amazing staff at CSULB, and most deserve better pay and treatment.
values.QID20_TEXT: Faculty and Administration treatment of Staff. Staff are often treated carelessly, especially by Faculty/Administration. Faculty and staff dynamics are not healthy in the College of Education, and Administration makes things more hostile with obvious favoritism of Faculty members over Staff.
values.QID21_TEXT: Faculty and Staff Relations. There needs to be more equity in fairness, openness for involvement in professional development and committee work, and general treatment.
values.QID29_TEXT: Better Pay, Remote Work, Similar Benefits and Scheduling for the new position. And I was too tired and emotionally exhausted to continue as Staff at CSULB.

values.QID19_TEXT: The staff. The coworkers in ATS are amazing people, and I will miss working with them. They are all highly competent and great at their jobs. They deserve more respect.
values.QID20_TEXT: I have so much to say but I will keep it to two points both of which are equity. When I worked in IR I had an interm director put me in a room and yell at me for an hour. It turns out he was upset by feedback he received from a department, but it was feedback on a role he completed. When I pointed that out (after an hour of not knowing why I was yelled at) he went to hit me. I moved and he punched the cabinet where my head was. I reported this to Marianne Hata and Dhushy and despite other reports of aggression by this employee I was told to "work it out" with him by being alone with him in another office. I still am haunted by this and was shown how little CSULB cares about staff. But I transferred to ATS and things were better until our new AVP. The second thing I liked about CSULB is that I came out as non-binary and even now two years later I am misgendered more then I am gendered correctly. I have been told that my pronouns (they/them) are just too hard for others to use. Even as I was leaving I was having to advocate for my correct pronoun usage. There are 43 reported gender neutral bathrooms on campus and I was only ever able to find one (the ones by the theater) and they were often locked. If it is this hard for me as a staff imagine how hard it is for students - and on that note I would hear transphobic comments just walking through the halls. Again, imagine what our trans students hear! Long Beach needs to be more transgender friendly, especially because the rest of the world isn't.
values.QID21_TEXT: I loved my job. I did not love how stressed I had to be and the expectation that I had to pull 180 hours a week of work to do everything on my PD and was told to just deal with it.
values.QID29_TEXT: My AVP

values.QID19_TEXT: I liked working on the boat and making educational social media posts

values.QID20_TEXT: I don't feel like I was compensated accordingly to my worth and what I brought into my position. I also don't feel like my growth was fostered by my managers.
values.QID21_TEXT: I would change the amount staff gets paid because there is so much turnover of good, quality people because of the lack of a livable wage. That is also one of the main reasons why I am leaving.

values.QID19_TEXT: The staff was friendly & there seemed to be a culture of wanting to help one another from most folks.
values.QID20_TEXT: The management at the highest level didn’t seem to listen anyone. It was a “do as I say” kind of old world thinking. They seemed like they were more interested in impressing other ABPs & the Provist than actually helping to encourage & cultivate the cultures of the department. This behavior brought morale down as is the reason why the department is hemorrhaging employees.
values.QID21_TEXT: I would hire leaders that avtuatcared about their employees instead of using words like “servant leadership” as buzz words, but that actually modeled a serving posture.
values.QID29_TEXT: I didn’t feel heard it valued. I have wide skill set & could be useful in many capacities. I created work for myself to supply faculty throughout the e pandemic & had several faculty members email our AVP about how they appreciated the work I did & out AVP never once said a word to me about it.bury he praises others for small tasks. It almost felt like it was personal. In addition, it was communicated to me by the ASM in a meeting with my colleagues that I would have certain employees reporting to me any more. Although I was ok with them not reporting to me, I thought the way it as communicated to me in front of my colleagues and all the upper management was bad form & made me feel like I didn’t matter. Lastly, I did some work that was being seen by other CSUs & various faculty to which our department got praise for & one of the directors in my department that I don’t even work for took credit for my work. There was no integrity to some of the management.

values.QID19_TEXT: I genuinely enjoyed working with my coworkers and my team. Shae Mitchell and Jen Dizon are brilliant and dedicated employees, along with being wonderful people. Working with the Systems team was also fantastic. HR was also a great resource for me. Everyone was kind and professional. I will miss working at this campus.
values.QID20_TEXT: ES Management-Meghan Griffith and Donna Green. Meghan was my supervisor and she is the reason I am leaving. Her overwhelmingly intense micromanaging and nitpicking caused me to have panic attacks and contributed to my medical situation. When I asked Donna back in February if I could return to my previous position in System, she said she needed me where I was (but could not stop me from applying) and had the position posting taken down. Then a few months later, the position was filled as an emergency hire. This felt like a sneaky betrayal, so what I liked least about working here is the management style in Enrollment Services.
values.QID21_TEXT: I would have a different Registrar, someone who is encouraging and supports their staff.
values.QID29_TEXT: My current director supervisor and the AVP.

values.QID19_TEXT: The teams I worked with are great and hard working individuals who truly care about the students they service.

values.QID29_TEXT: I was informed by my supervisor that my contract will not be getting renewed even though I was never given any feedback or evaluation of my work.

values.QID21_TEXT: There needs to be greater intention in bringing diversity to staff and faculty across the university. The workplace should be a safe place for everyone and a person should not have to fear retaliation or management apathy when faced with racist or hostile circumstances. Also, there should be different levels to certain positions especially when comparing jobs to size of departments that are being served. For example, a department coordinator supporting a department of 15 faculty should not be paid the same as one who supports a department of 90 faculty.
values.QID29_TEXT: Lack of equitable pay and advanced employment opportunities. I could not live on what I was being paid. There seemed no way for me to advance in my position as an ASC 1 even though I earned both a bachelor's and master's degree during my tenure as department coordinator.

values.QID20_TEXT: The environment was very quiet, any socializing was discouraged which I've always found to be a necessary part of a desk job that can be slow at times.  It's somewhat ironic that the pay is so low at a progressive university that hopes to balance the lifestyle of its residents as a whole.  The pay isn't enough to stay above water in southern California, even when taking into account the exceptional health benefits.
values.QID21_TEXT: There can easily be less jobs, but with higher wages for those with jobs.  Many people have a lot of downtime here.  I would also think a more lively environment where people can socialize a bit and not sit in silence all day would be beneficial for productivity and work culture.

values.QID19_TEXT: It was the highest paying role I've had in my career so far.
values.QID20_TEXT: Being micromanaged and not trusted to bring my years of expertise and experience to help build out the program in ways I knew I could. There was also a ton of workplace politics in play that just detracted from actual work being done and overall sense of productivity and efficiency.
values.QID21_TEXT: N/A
values.QID29_TEXT: Not feeling trusted to pursue initiatives and work that I know would benefit my department and the workplace politics of administration.

values.QID19_TEXT: I feel a real sense of community at The Beach.  I felt like all of the people I worked with directly as well as outside of BMAC were dedicated professionals who valued inclusivity in the student experience.  My DSA colleagues go above and beyond to provide equitable experiences for students at The Beach.
values.QID20_TEXT: I felt my wages were stagnant with little way to gain upward mobility and economic advancement in my professional and my personal life.  This is especially demotivating as an alum of The Beach which takes pride in providing upward mobility to graduates.
values.QID21_TEXT: Provide more equitable, not equal, experiences for staff and faculty
values.QID29_TEXT: The closing of the child care center on the campus, with little notice, was the impetuous to leaving my current role.  With such short notice my family was unable to secure high quality, affordable, care with only 6 months notice.  I love my current role and the dept I work for - I was willing to sacrifice upward mobility for the convince of child care on the campus and the high caliber of care offered.

values.QID19_TEXT: What i liked most working here is showing up and knowing how much I've impacted student's lives. I also made great connections with some people on campus who drove me to improve my skills daily
values.QID20_TEXT: I came in with a high expectations of great teamwork and comradery to end up feeling depleted every time I talk to someone from upper management. Most of them are looking out for themselves and does not show any genuine interest in the staff members.
values.QID21_TEXT: I think CSULB offers a lot of programs, it's the staff members responsibility to make the effort into participating
values.QID29_TEXT: My main reason is due to financial reason, lack of leadership involvement , mentorship, and how much it does not matter of the work you've done, you are temporary to leadership.

values.QID19_TEXT: One of the things I enjoyed most about working at the Beach was the positive relationships I was able to build with my colleagues. I had the opportunity to work with a diverse group of individuals who were all passionate about the work we were doing, and it was a pleasure to collaborate with them. We were able to build a strong sense of camaraderie and teamwork, which made the work environment a positive and supportive one. While there were certainly challenges in the job, I always appreciated the opportunity to work with such talented and dedicated people. I also appreciated the workshops Stacey Schack provided; they were informative and intentional.
values.QID20_TEXT: One aspect of my previous role that I found challenging was the lack of autonomy as a manager. My supervisor tended to micromanage, which made it difficult for me to fully utilize my skills and take ownership of my responsibilities. I understand the importance of guidance and support, but giving managers more trust and flexibility to make decisions would boost morale and lead to more effective and efficient outcomes. In the future, I would appreciate working in an environment that encourages autonomy and empowers managers to make decisions based on their expertise and judgment.
values.QID21_TEXT: Establish mentoring programs that pair employees with mentors who can provide guidance and support, especially for employees from underrepresented groups.
values.QID29_TEXT: I made the difficult decision to leave my position due to mental health concerns caused by a toxic work environment. My boss's micromanaging behavior and passive-aggressive communication style created a tense and stressful working environment that negatively impacted my mental health. Additionally, my boss frequently attacked my management style, making it difficult for me to feel confident in my abilities and effectively contribute to the team. While I recognize that every manager has their own management style, I felt that the behavior I experienced was not conducive to a positive work environment. It was ultimately in my best interest to prioritize my mental health and seek a more supportive and collaborative work environment where I can thrive both personally and professionally.

values.QID19_TEXT: I went to school here and also worked here as a student assistant, so it was nice to continue on into my first full-time professional staff role here as well. The networking opportunities it provided me was amazing.
values.QID20_TEXT: I feel that with the position I held I did not see myself growing or advancing and therefore applied to another institution in order to achieve that.
values.QID21_TEXT: I really like how CSULB is having a Pride Celebration for all staff, faculty, and students on June 27th. I feel as though most events are catered towards students and staff/faculty are not usually encouraged to attend. I feel as though staff/faculty should be encouraged to engage more in the community and attend diversity and inclusion related events on campus.
values.QID29_TEXT: I want to be more involved within Student Affairs and felt that there were no open or available position for me to move into here at CSULB. Additionally, I plan on completing graduate school at the new institution I will be working for.

values.QID19_TEXT: The children, my co-workers and the all around beauty of Isabel Patterson CDC. The benefits are awesome as well.
values.QID20_TEXT: Things didn't get fixed in a timely manner. It took CSULB 4 years to finally properly fix my kitchen freezer.
values.QID21_TEXT: Biological Women Only Spaces
values.QID29_TEXT: Retiring

values.QID19_TEXT: great dept chair and co-workers
values.QID20_TEXT: lack of consistent job training
values.QID21_TEXT: none
values.QID29_TEXT: leaving for a less stressful job that pays more

values.QID19_TEXT: My colleagues have been wonderful and the campus is a beautiful place to work. I have also enjoyed attending many performances at the Cole Conservatory. My work has been gratifying and I feel I have made a difference for students and faculty in the two colleges I serve.
values.QID20_TEXT: The bureaucracy! Especially the tendency to interpret regulations overly narrowly and to make changes in processes without sufficient input from end-users. We talk a lot  about celebrating diversity on campus but we spend a lot of time trying to make everything cookie cutter.
values.QID21_TEXT: I don't have any recommendations.
values.QID29_TEXT: I am retiring.

values.QID19_TEXT: The diversity of the campus is wonderful. The team I worked with (University Relations & Development) had a great culture, everyone was extremely kind, and I did not sense any "drama."
values.QID20_TEXT: I did not like my role. It was quite different than what I expected (not that the job description was incorrect) but the everyday work did not end up being something I felt passionate about. I was also a team of one, so with being a new employee and working remotely 4+ days a week, it felt like I was on an island alone.
values.QID21_TEXT: No recommendations - I really appreciated the reading group for How to Be an Ally.
values.QID29_TEXT: I am returning to my previous employer in a new role.

values.QID19_TEXT: The atmosphere
values.QID20_TEXT: The attitudes
values.QID21_TEXT: NA
values.QID29_TEXT: Contact ending

values.QID19_TEXT: I loved being able to help students and changes lives for the better. As a CSULB Alumni with school spirit, I was ready to represent as a proud employee at The Beach. I loved being back on campus, guiding new students and providing a positive experience. I loved utilizing my previous skills, gaining and implementing acquired skills to help my team members and lighten the load for my immediate supervisors. Working remotely was quite enjoyable as well. I'm grateful for the people who actually tried to protect me when I was forced to endure unacceptable behavior. When I wasn't being targeted, I loved being able to take walks around the campus. The fresh air was very nice on a sunny day. There are some amazing people on campus who really make a difference and create moments of kindness. I loved participating in the "Day at the Beach" event. I was able to give a positive outlook on my time as a student and provide tips to academic success which was well received.
values.QID20_TEXT: I wish I had felt protected as an employee or even a human being. The amount of injustice and lack of neutrality spoke volumes. I experienced bullying/intimidation, microaggressions, frequent misidentification, the complete disregard for others, hostile work environment and lack of respect. My experience impacted my mental health, physical health and how I view the workforce as a CSULB Alumni who is a Black woman with a disability. Due to the incidents, I started having panic attacks and sought help because of the constant fear/anxiety and despair from how I was being treated by specific individuals. I did not like how my feelings were invalidated and silenced by specific individuals. I did not appreciate how some of the supervisors were being immensely disrespected. The mishandling of incidents was undeniably troubling to say the least. However, I would like to believe that all of these situations can be avoidable in the future but only if people are willing to do the work. In the end, we must remember how every employee that comes into contact with a student is a role model in one way or another. This should be the same from one colleague to another. Each employee should demonstrate compassion for each other because everyone's main goal should be to work towards student success as a team.
values.QID21_TEXT: I would recommend interactive trainings/programs regarding unconscious/conscious biased, discrimination, microaggressions, bullying, conflict resolution, and how to not create a hostile work environment. I also believe bringing back Stacey Schack would greatly benefit the department.
values.QID29_TEXT: I was informed that my classification no longer exists. So, they were not in a position to renew.

values.QID19_TEXT: The wonderful staff of the CNSM and the opportunity to share all the resources the SLC has to offer with the campus and our community!
values.QID20_TEXT: I hope someday to see the SLC coordinator position become a full time position so that we can accept all the requests we had to provide resources and programming for our campus and our communities.
values.QID21_TEXT: More opportunities to network with the other colleges
values.QID29_TEXT: I completed the teacher credential program and am hired on as a classroom teacher in a full time position.

values.QID20_TEXT: The university's inability to create a sense of belonging for Black staff, faculty, and students. The failure to create opportunities for growth, development, and promotion of Black staff and faculty. The university often fails to address the inequities in the hiring and retention of Black faculty and staff and the support of Black students.
values.QID21_TEXT: There are many programs/initiative available, however I believe the failure is in the lack of implementation, tracking, and follow through to ensure the application of the identified DEI practices. Going through the "motions" does not effect change.

values.QID29_TEXT: Retirement due to lack of compensation advancement, salary didn't keep up with inflation let alone compensate for performance or experience.

values.QID19_TEXT: I enjoyed the closeness of the CLA Dean's office and the leaders I learned from. The campus is also a beautiful place to work.
values.QID20_TEXT: The commute and the hierarchy. It was difficult to move up independently unless my supervisor vouched for me. It was also difficult to hear from AVP, VP and higher ups in general when I needed their approval or help. Also, when I was a coordonator, my job duties and tasks did not reflect my PD in reality and i felt very underpaid for the amount of work that I did.
values.QID21_TEXT: More workshops or programs for men about gender inequalitiy and sexual harrassment.
values.QID29_TEXT: Better opportunity to move up to a higher position and earn higher pay at another CSU.

values.QID20_TEXT: Relationship with manager. Not a cohesiveness amongst departments to understand what each department does. Time not valued, lots of non-replies. Not open to new ideas or change.
values.QID21_TEXT: Background inclusion lacked here. Needs diversity in regard to workers with different education and skill backgrounds.
values.QID29_TEXT: Terminated with no explanation.

values.QID19_TEXT: I was a first-year student in 1990, graduating from Long Beach Poly High School, and placed on probation after I found out I was pregnant. Unfortunately, I was disqualified. I started a new journey at LBCC, received my AA degree, and transferred back to CSULB in 2002 when I began my professional journey as a Student Affairs practitioner with ASI. After a 20-year journey, I obtained my bachelor's degree and continue to receive my master's degree. I had to share that story to understand what I like most about working at Long Beach State, initially as a Long Beach native. After 21 years at CSULB, the students we served are what I like most about working at the Beach, and the best environment to work in was my 14 years working with Associated Students, Incorporated.
values.QID20_TEXT: As an adult learner, after I received my master's degree, I desired to be in a more direct student-facing role, use my counseling degree to support students, improve their overall experience, and ultimately become the Dean of Students. However, unbeknownst to me, I learned the dark side of leadership and its negative impact on staff. For the record, I did not know Carmen Taylor before her tenure at the Beach and did my due diligence to understand who she was (her character) before I did an informal interview with her as an assignment for my class in the SDHE program. Like other Black women, I was excited to see a Black woman in a high and visible role at the Beach. Based on that informal interview, she informed me that positions would be forthcoming in Housing and Residential Life and asked if I was interested in applying. She said there are no guarantees if you apply, you will have to go through the process. I shared I would review the position description and decided to go for it because of my work with Dr. Caveness's Title IX respondent program and my interest in student conduct. What I liked least about working here was the traumatic experience in Housing and Residential Life and working in a toxic environment in Student Affairs after that. It is important to note I did not feel the toxicity in other areas. I had a great working relationship with many folks outside of Student Affairs.
values.QID21_TEXT: To be a model institution in the CSU system, CSULB leadership has to be honest with the climate and culture. All students, staff, faculty, and administrators have different experiences. Listen to them all and address them accordingly; then, over time, hopefully, it will change. VP Doug Robinson always said it is about the people. People can make the experience memorable or forgettable. Stop changing the narrative when folks share their experience. Also, leadership, stop talking about other leaders in front of folks, and stop gossiping (loose lips). It is damaging and reveals your character and if folks can trust you. Lastly, please be are careful who is conducting DEIA training. Colleagues will not trust the person or process if they are selective in who they work with and greet. Remember, the small things matter to many colleagues on campus.
values.QID29_TEXT: I am leaving my current position mainly because of the toxic work environment I have worked in for the past five years.  I was removed from Housing and Residential Life. I was not looking for a new opportunity and was on medical leave or vacation when my position was posted for an open recruitment search. Colleagues called me asking me where I was going. I had no idea what they were referring to. I had no choice in the matter but to accept the position that was created to cover up the removal. I remember crying in the parking lot as I walked to that interview with Travis, Rafael, and another colleague serving on the search committee. I had to call a friend to help me calm down as I had a panic attack. Corry said it was Carmen, and she said that I was not in Corry's vision for the future. Leadership blaming each other. Can you even imagine what that does to an employee? No. I had to deal with the gossip and folks attempting to damage my reputation based on their assumption of what had happened. Simple answer: I was in survival mode to ensure I had a job to care for myself and my family.  Then, I had to move under the leadership of Dr. John Hamilton, and in our first meeting at the Chartroom, he said, "Are you going to be a troublemaker like Brett Waterfield and Ms. Robinson? I had no idea what he was referring to, as Brett was a friend of mine, and I was aware of how Carmen's administration treated him. That indicated respecting him as my immediate supervisor but keep your head down and do your job. The first year was shaky, as the position was not defined and was only created to accommodate my removal from Housing and Residential - classification and salary.  Then, Dr. Takemoto asked me if I would be interested in overseeing the Women's and Gender Equity Center. I was beyond excited because, since the traumatic experience, there was a focus and a more defined role. The scope of the Women's and Gender Equity Center was not of interest to Dr. Hamilton; therefore, I had more autonomy and less micromanagement. The only challenge was my former coordinator.  Then, Dr. Hamilton's area underwent a reorganization—I was appointed Senior Director of Multicultural Affairs and Inclusion. We found out in November 2020, and it became effective January 2021. I was inheriting a department where the OMA Director was still in his role, a new OMA director search was happening simultaneously, onboarding a new OMA Assistant Director for the Black Resource Center, understanding the Dream Success Center, and still dealing with challenges of WGEC staffing. It was a mess again that I inherited. I did the best I could in that first year.  Another reason why I am leaving is the Women's and Gender Equity Center was included in the OMA Director's position description. When I asked Claudia Plaza why I was over the WGEC, she said it was an error and would change it; however, it was never changed and was included in the position description for the recently hired OMA Director. That is a public disrespectful message that I am not a welcomed or valued member of the Student Affairs team as the Director of the Women's and Gender Equity Center. Another reason I asked is if this reorganization includes a pay increase with the increase in scope. Claudia shared after a six or eight-month delay that, there would not be an increase because my position was never at the level to warrant an increase. That tells me that I will never receive an increase in my salary, will never get hired or thought of for another position on campus, and I am still being punished for what she and Carmen did creating the position in 2018. Further, removing OMA and then supervisory duties from my portfolio without justification sent the campus community a message that I failed as a leader. It further hindered my professional growth and mental health.  Lastly, I am leaving because of the lack of communication and abuse of power in leadership. Three colleagues who abused their power under Carmen Taylor's administration have left, and the one, Claudia Plaza, who remains, has continued to be a problem for me and others. Her leadership style is messy, harms folks, and takes no accountability when she or her team makes a mistake. I cannot work in that environment anymore. I do not deserve the treatment. I have been a loyal employee for 21 years. Advocating for yourself is not a reason to treat folks differently. Colleagues are leaving for a reason, which is not always the person. Take heed and realize the system is flawed; people like Claudia Plaza make it up with their power of influence. I have nothing against Claudia personally; however, in her role, she must always be ethical, not selective in who she wants to help (double standard), or become creative in her strategies to force folks to decide to leave the institution.   Other reasons have inflicted unnecessary pain and frustration, too many changes, and finding out on Monday that I have to meet with colleagues about my retirement, vacation, sick leave, etc, CSULB, you have to do more to care about your employees. You cannot create a wellness model that only applies to our students; please remember your staff, faculty, and administrators need empathy and compassion but, most importantly, need to be respected and valued. I always showed Pam Rayburn respect. It is a choice we all have to make. I was labeled and, through leadership actions, realized there was no further interest in my personhood. I love the Beach; unfortunately, at this time, the Beach does not appreciate the value and impact I contribute daily to the student population we serve. I know my worth and humbly removed myself from the equation because I am not done yet. My goal was to retire from CSULB.

values.QID20_TEXT: The lack of any automated processes for student or staff timesheets, meaning math mistakes made by a lowest-rung, part-time coordinator (me) could affect student employee pay. Lack of repeat trainings. My manager's lack of training. Lack of comprehensive HR guides or self-training resources at CHHS. Siloed communication both between the garden and the deans office, and within the garden department (only one employee working per day. physical distance from all campus contacts). Indefensible and ineffective policies regarding what visitors can wear in the garden during public hours.
values.QID21_TEXT: Before our latest Administrative Coordinator was hired, who is native Japanese, every Japanese Garden supervisor was white (including me). I am not a D&I professional, but the lack of attention and support for the garden from the university in general means that hiring decisions have been made quickly and desperately since my manager started at the garden. No cultural considerations can be adequately made while that's the case, and the garden bills itself as a cultural focal point on campus.

values.QID19_TEXT: Firstly, being a part of the university's initiatives (GI 2023, Eliminating Barriers and Beach 2030) and contributing to the betterment of the community we serve has been incredibly fulfilling. The sense of purpose and the positive impact we make on the lives of students and the broader community have been a driving force in my dedication to the organization.  Furthermore, the camaraderie and collaboration among my co-workers have been exceptional. My team, peers and external partners have made it my experience here truly great. I have had the privilege of working with talented and passionate individuals who have not only been great colleagues but some have also turned into friends.
values.QID20_TEXT: I appreciated my time here at the Beach, but one area I found challenging was the pace at which projects and goals are expected to be completed by staff and implemented by managers. As managers we have to set and create reasonable goals, expectations and deadlines to foster an engaging and motivating work environment. However, jumping from project to project (without pausing to celebrate wins, or clearly stating priorities) can create a work environment that feels "go,go,go" at all times which can ultimately lead to people burn out. I understand we don't control directives from the CO or legislation, but we can control the delivery to our staff, assign priorities and meaningfully ackowldge wins and/or successful completion of projects. I believe a more adaptable approach would enhance both employee motivation and productivity.
values.QID21_TEXT: Firstly, I would encourage Mentorship and Networking Programs. Establish mentorship programs that connect employees with mentors from diverse backgrounds, providing guidance and support for professional development. Facilitate networking events that encourage employees to connect across different departments, fostering a sense of community and collaboration. Secondly, I would recommend Diversity Training and Workshops. Implement regular diversity training sessions for all employees to raise awareness and promote understanding of different cultures, perspectives, and experiences. Offer both leaders and staff workshops on unconscious bias to help employees recognize and address implicit biases that may affect decision-making. Third, Cultural Competency Training. Provide training to enhance cultural competency among employees, emphasizing the importance of understanding and respecting different cultural backgrounds. Lastly, Regular Feedback and Surveys. Conduct regular surveys to gather feedback from employees on their experiences related to diversity and inclusion. Use this feedback to make informed decisions and continuously improve the workplace culture.
values.QID29_TEXT: I have made the decision to leave my current position to pursue a better opportunity that aligns with my career goals. This new opportunity offers a more competitive compensation package, increased flexibility in terms of work hours, and the option for remote work. I appreciate the experiences and learning opportunities gained during my time here and am excited about the prospects ahead.

values.QID19_TEXT: The Campus and my coworkers
values.QID20_TEXT: Flexibility of work hours and recent lack of childcare during school year and summer for staff
values.QID21_TEXT: Please bring back Campus daycare/preschool (Isabel Patterson is geared toward students) and bring back the Academic Enrichment Summer camp.
values.QID29_TEXT: For a promotion

values.QID19_TEXT: Helping the campus end-users

values.QID21_TEXT: That does not really apply to my position but I do think there is little room for growth in most positions and it seems as though the University does not mind losing qualified people if it means they need to pay them more money
values.QID29_TEXT: Frustration with the current Risk Management wanting the buyers to do their job. Frustration with Contract Management not reviewing terms in a timely manner.

values.QID19_TEXT: I liked being one of the few employees with a true office (four walls; not a cubical)
values.QID20_TEXT: The lack of institutional support for the program I was working for was apparent from day-1, however, it was not made clear during the interview or the salary negotiations. I also do not like the lack of clarity in communication from upper administration. I was promised a teaching opportunity and to receive a pay increase from union negotiations because my classification. Neither of those things came to fruition in my time here. Furthermore, unlike other institutions where meetings with VPs would be honored, I was given several barriers, and workarounds to never meet with the VP and as such I had no understanding of what the goal of my program was, because under both managers I worked for, that was never clear.
values.QID21_TEXT: Creating professional development tracks for upward mobility is the best way to increase diversity at CSULB. However, CSULB would also need to create infrastructure to support diverse staff instead of just hiring mediocre people of color. Promote within. There a A LOT of highly intelligent, forward-thinking, individuals here at the Beach from diverse backgrounds who cannot advance because of red-tape, antiquated philosophies (people not being able to gain promotion to an SSP IV because they are an SSP II — which this is the only campus I’ve seen that happen). There are great people here, stop ignoring them.
values.QID29_TEXT: There are a lot of reasons but the biggest reason is the lack of follow through from promises made prior to my starting my role here at CSULB. As noted in a previous question I was made several promises: a higher salary, a pay increase, and the opportunity to lecture here. I do recognize that is my fault for not vetting the “sounds too good to be true” of the job offer, but upon making my decision I feel certain. I also didn’t appreciate being told that ‘you don’t want more money, you just need more motivation’ by the head of HR when I was expressing my concerns about my lower-wages and how that impacted my struggle to pay bills. Of course working here I thought I would be making more money and have greater opportunity to pay off debt. That was not the case, and the assertion that ‘motivation’ for the work was the issue felt like a slap in the face.

values.QID19_TEXT: What I enjoy and love most about working here is the people I got to work with whether it was my team or collaborating with a different unit outside of my team. People in ES make me feel so welcome and at home. Although my tenure here is only 2 years, I feel as though I have known so many people here for so much longer. The support and kindness that is emulated and shared here is something I have never felt before anywhere else. The people here that I have met and grown so close to truly deserve the best and so much more than what is provided here for them. Despite not receiving livable wages and lack of flexibility from management even though these folks are bombarded with so many tasks and projects, people still come in every single day to excel at their job and provide top notch services to students every single day. I have the utmost respect and so much love for these folks who have made my time here so wonderful and supported me so much, especially during my toughest days at work.
values.QID20_TEXT: What I didn’t like was how management treats all of us working in Enrollment Services. I don’t mean management in my immediate lead and manager. I mean management that is above them at the director and AVP level. For starters, flexibility and support is supposedly offered here and is expressed from management that we reach out if we need anything. All the times I have done so, no support or flexibility would be provided. For example, I requested to switch my remote computer desktop to a laptop as my WFH desk became too small for a computer. Although there is a plethora of laptops in Enrollment Services available for me to switch. This request was denied immediately and I was told that a computer desktop is needed for my “eye health” as the screens are bigger than a laptop. Others spoke to me and said they have a laptop provided by ES for remote work and in no way is there eye health any worse than mine from using it. That is just one example out of so many that I have faced here and all I ask for is flexibility and support so I can do my job to the best of my ability. Additionally, important teams in ES, such as my modules team, are severely understaffed and this is known. Although this is the case, projects and requests from management continue to be stacked on our teams with fast approaching deadlines and pressure from management to hurry and get such tasks complete. While it is understandable that many projects and requests are important, management does not create a constructed timeline for these items and expects us to complete all that is required immediately and perfectly on top of our other normal tasks and processes we have day to day. What would be helpful is to plan ahead for these pressing projects so teams have an ample amount of time to do the work. Furthermore, while employees are seen getting reprimanded constantly for actions such as walking to the other side of the office to talk to their colleagues, management does not receive similar treatment despite having numerous complaints made against them. The University Registrar has multiple complaints of being difficult to work with, lack of communication skills, and passive aggressive towards their team. Folks have gone to the ASM of ES to only be told that the University Registrar is trying their best yet there have been no changes from them and have also been told that this may not be a good fit for them to work here and have been presented with other job postings to apply to. All these complaints yet there have been no solutions and it has only gotten worse now with almost 9 vacancies (and counting) under the University Registrar. The problem here is not the people, the problem is management needs to change and if nothing happens, it can 100% be expected that more good people will leave CSULB and may even never come back into higher education.
values.QID21_TEXT: Provide staff with time off of work to do DEI training and workshops to better support this value. With staff always working, there is never anytime to actually do these programs/initiatives. Make it an effort to have time off of work such as every 1st Friday there will be a DEI workshop and all staff need to take 3 hours off in the day for it.
values.QID29_TEXT: The main reason I am leaving is because of management, specifically because I can no longer work under the University Registrar. They have made much of my time here more difficult than it should be with denying security requests that external users are in need of in order to do their job. Multiple times, requests have been denied with no reason and many external users end up reaching out to me wondering why they don’t have access. My team was never provided with protocol on communication to users who get their access denied and in no way are we allowed to say who denied it and provide them the reason for denial. Even if I reached out to the external user’s supervisor requesting for a business justification for the need, they will still deny the request and disable the user from performing their job at the top level. Additionally, the University Registrar has chosen to take passive aggressive approaches with replying back to emails at times if ever I didn’t include a specific item on a report write up and/or didn’t include the correct people on an email memo. While I know I too am always learning at my job, in no way should anyone receive that treatment from their director. Furthermore, the University Registrar does not take initiative to reach out to frustrated external users and rather expects their team to take care of the situation, even though the user wants to hear from the Registrar. While I do not have a problem with confrontation, it is not my job to address concerns that need to be handled by the University Registrar.
